message.content:
## Source Extractor

Research question: "Best algorithms/techniques to make audio the most comprehensible (e.g. time-scale modification like WSOLA/PSOLA, intelligibility enhancement, silence/pause compression, spectral and prosody processing), AND how "speed listening" works at playback speeds greater than 16x — the perceptual limits of accelerated speech comprehension, training/adaptation effects, and the signal-processing methods that make extreme speed-ups intelligible."

Fetch and extract key claims from this source:
**URL:** https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC3745084/
**Title:** How can audiovisual pathways enhance the temporal resolution of time-compressed speech in blind subjects? (Hertrich et al., Frontiers in Psychology 2013)
**Found via:** training and adaptation in blind listeners search

## Task
1. Use WebFetch to retrieve the page content.
2. Assess source quality: primary research/institution? secondary reporting? blog/opinion? forum? unreliable?
3. Extract 2-5 FALSIFIABLE claims that bear on the research question. Each claim must:
   - be a concrete, checkable statement (not vague generalities)
   - include a direct quote from the source as support
   - be rated central/supporting/tangential to the research question
4. Note publish date if available.

If the fetch fails or the page is irrelevant/paywalled, return claims: [] and sourceQuality: "unreliable".

Structured output only.

attachment.content:
- caveman: Ultra-compressed communication mode. Cuts token usage ~75% by speaking like caveman while keeping full technical accuracy. Supports intensity levels: lite, full (default), ultra, wenyan-lite, wenyan-full, wenyan-ultra. Use when user says "caveman mode", "talk like caveman", "use caveman", "less tokens", "be brief", or invokes /caveman. Also auto-triggers when token efficiency is requested.
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- frontend-design:frontend-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use this skill when the user asks to build web components, pages, or applications. Generates creative, polished code that avoids generic AI aesthetics.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: I'll fetch the source and extract the relevant claims.

input.prompt: Extract key findings about: time-compressed speech comprehension, perceptual limits of accelerated speech, training/adaptation effects in blind vs sighted listeners, the role of audiovisual/visual cortex pathways in enhancing temporal resolution of fast speech, and any specific speed/rate numbers (syllables per second, words per minute, compression factors). Provide direct quotes for each finding. Also note the publication date and journal.

content.content:
# Key Findings on Time-Compressed Speech Comprehension

**Publication:** Front Psychol, August 16, 2013

## Speech Comprehension Speed Limits

**Normal speech rate:** "the normal range of 6 syllables/s" and separately "normal speaking rate amounts to only 4–8 syllables/s"

**Ultra-fast comprehension in blind subjects:** "blind subjects can learn to use accelerated text-to-speech systems for 'reading' texts at ultra-fast speaking rates (>16 syllables/s)"

## The "Bottleneck" Problem

The authors identify a critical constraint: "the 'bottleneck' for understanding time-compressed speech seems related to higher demands for buffering phonological material and is, presumably, linked to frontal brain structures."

More specifically in sighted individuals: "the 'bottleneck' in sighted subjects for the comprehension of time-compressed speech arises from limited temporary storage capacities for phonological materials rather than speed constraints"

## Visual Cortex Enhancement Mechanism

In blind listeners, activation patterns show: "this ability, among other brain regions, significantly covaries with BOLD responses in bilateral pulvinar, right visual cortex, and left supplementary motor area."

The proposed pathway involves: "blind subjects seem to be able to recruit part of their visual cortex – presumably via subcortical afferent auditory information – to represent this prosodic information"

## Critical Temporal Resolution Finding

MEG evidence demonstrates: "a particular component in right occipital cortex phase-locked to the syllable onsets of accelerated speech" with latencies of "40–80 ms," indicating early subcortical processing rather than higher-order cognitive mechanisms.

content.text: Let me get more detail on the training/adaptation aspects and the specific mechanisms.

input.prompt: Focus on: (1) How blind subjects train/adapt to ultra-fast speech and how much faster than sighted people they can comprehend. (2) The specific role of the pulvinar and subcortical pathways in tracking syllable onsets. (3) Any statement about prosody/syllable onset markers aiding comprehension. (4) Whether the visual cortex provides a timing/temporal "marker" or "anchor" for parsing fast speech. Give exact quotes. Also identify the authors and whether this is a primary research/review article in a peer-reviewed journal.

content.content:
# Analysis of Audiovisual Pathways and Time-Compressed Speech in Blind Subjects

**Article Type & Authors:**
This is a peer-reviewed hypothesis and theory paper by Ingo Hertrich, Susanne Dietrich, and Hermann Ackermann, published in *Frontiers in Psychology* (2013).

---

## (1) Training/Adaptation and Speed Capabilities

Blind subjects can learn to comprehend dramatically faster speech than sighted individuals:

> "blind subjects can learn to use accelerated text-to-speech systems for 'reading' texts at ultra-fast speaking rates (>16 syllables/s), exceeding by far the normal range of 6 syllables/s"

The paper notes this ability develops in both early and late blind individuals, suggesting reliance on pathways present in sighted people but unavailable for ultra-fast processing in sighted individuals.

---

## (2) Pulvinar and Subcortical Pathways

The authors propose a specific subcortical mechanism:

> "auditory timing information is derived from the acoustic signal at a pre-cortical stage, presumably, at the level of the SC, and then transferred to visual cortex via pulvinar"

The pulvinar's role involves gating: the pathway exhibits "a powerful gating function on visual cortex activity."

---

## (3) Prosody/Syllable Onset Markers

MEG evidence demonstrates direct syllable tracking:

> "a particular component in right occipital cortex phase-locked to the syllable onsets of accelerated speech"

The latency (40-80ms) indicates early, signal-driven processing rather than higher-order linguistic operations.

---

## (4) Temporal "Marker" or "Anchor" Function

The authors explicitly propose a timing mechanism:

> "visual cortex might consist in the transformation of the received timing signal into a series of (syllabic) events"

This allows blind subjects to overcome the auditory system's temporal constraints by "outsourcing" syllable timing to visual cortex.

content.text: I have enough detail to extract falsifiable claims. This is a primary, peer-reviewed hypothesis/theory paper from Frontiers in Psychology (2013) by Hertrich, Dietrich, and Ackermann.

claims.claim: Blind subjects can learn to comprehend accelerated text-to-speech at ultra-fast rates exceeding 16 syllables per second, far above the normal speech range of ~6 syllables/s (normal speaking rate 4-8 syllables/s).
claims.quote: blind subjects can learn to use accelerated text-to-speech systems for 'reading' texts at ultra-fast speaking rates (>16 syllables/s), exceeding by far the normal range of 6 syllables/s

claims.claim: The bottleneck for understanding time-compressed speech in sighted listeners is limited temporary phonological storage capacity (buffering), not a hard speed constraint, and is linked to frontal brain structures.
claims.quote: the 'bottleneck' in sighted subjects for the comprehension of time-compressed speech arises from limited temporary storage capacities for phonological materials rather than speed constraints

claims.claim: In blind listeners, ultra-fast speech comprehension recruits visual cortex, with the ability covarying with BOLD responses in bilateral pulvinar, right visual cortex, and left supplementary motor area.
claims.quote: this ability, among other brain regions, significantly covaries with BOLD responses in bilateral pulvinar, right visual cortex, and left supplementary motor area

claims.claim: A right occipital-cortex MEG component phase-locks to syllable onsets of accelerated speech at short latencies (40-80 ms), indicating an early subcortical/signal-driven timing mechanism rather than higher-order cognition.
claims.quote: a particular component in right occipital cortex phase-locked to the syllable onsets of accelerated speech

claims.claim: The proposed mechanism transfers auditory timing extracted at a pre-cortical stage (superior colliculus) to visual cortex via the pulvinar, where it is converted into a series of discrete syllabic timing events that anchor parsing of fast speech.
claims.quote: auditory timing information is derived from the acoustic signal at a pre-cortical stage, presumably, at the level of the SC, and then transferred to visual cortex via pulvinar

content.text: Extracted 5 falsifiable claims from this primary, peer-reviewed hypothesis/theory paper (Hertrich, Dietrich & Ackermann, Frontiers in Psychology, 16 Aug 2013). The source is directly relevant to the "speed listening >16x" portion of the research question, providing the key data point that trained blind listeners comprehend ultra-fast TTS at >16 syllables/s (vs. normal ~6) and a neural-mechanism account (visual-cortex recruitment via a subcortical SC→pulvinar pathway phase-locking to syllable onsets) for how extreme speed-ups become intelligible.
